sentences.text: I was-- I went through this one time.
It was at the Jewish Center.
We had the dispatch came for an interview, and it was me and Alfred Tibor.
You heard of him?
Yeah.
And we went through about 2 hours, 2 and 1/2 hours of this whole thing-- interviews and all this-- had on TV.
You blinked your eyes, and we were gone.
[LAUGHTER] Oh, I was furious.
Less than that.
Might as well get comfortable.
This all right, or?
I was born in 1935, to the best of my knowledge, [INAUDIBLE] from where in Europe.
As far as the dates are concerned, my mother tells me somewhere around that time, but to the best of my knowledge, it was 1935 in October.
I was born in a city called Trochenbrod.
It's actually, it's Poland.
It's up near Ukraine.
The countries have changed so many times during my lifetime that we figure it is basically [NON-ENGLISH].
And it was a small little town.
In Jewish, they called it Trochenbrod.
Basically, it's because our city was full of [YIDDISH], [YIDDISH] meaning mud.
And that's where you got it in as trochen, the town, and [YIDDISH], meaning mud.
We didn't have any-- any paved streets or anything, but it was a big, long street.

And in Polish or Ukrainian they called it Zofjowka, and it laid near our-- our-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] capital, or wherever we went to-- to register at a hospital was either Lutsk, [NON-ENGLISH], or Rowne.
Rowne, which was about, to the best of my figurings, around 350 to 400 kilometers.
Lutsk, we used to-- that was the big city.
That's where your hospitals, your records were kept.
And that was Lutsk.
And [NON-ENGLISH] was a little bit closer, but [NON-ENGLISH] was where we used to go to the next big town to catch trains or to deal the market.
That was already the big city to us.
And it lays near Katowice, which is a big city, and that's [INAUDIBLE].
And it was basically an all-Jewish town, a shtetl.
My family life in-- in Polish, in Poland, where I come from, the shtetl was real simple.
We had-- I'll give you more or less a description of it.
It was-- it's a one-street town.
You go, and it was a thoroughfare.
And that was like-- Columbus, Ohio, I would compare it to Route 40, which it would start at one end of the United States and go to the other one.
Trochenbrod was a-- was set around that street.
So what we had is houses on one side, and houses on the other side, and your land, or the fields, used to be in the back of the house, as far as your eyes could see.
That was your land, and your house was in the front.
And then the richer people or whatever would have some fields on the outskirts.
Now, we were surrounded with [NON-ENGLISH], villages, Polish and Ukraines, which were-- used to be surrounded around us, and we were right sort of in the middle.
And we had three, about five schuls in our town.
The houses were pretty far away.
And our house was just about in the middle of the town.

Like the post office was almost right across the street from us, And the city well, which most of our people from there used to go, and you'd have to go-- take the [YIDDISH], they'd call it in Yiddish, which is a well.
You used to hook the bucket up, and drop it down, and had to go churn it back out and bring it up.
Bring up your water, and then carry it on those things.
And this was almost like across the street.
We had a double, our house, and we had-- on one side, we had a [NON-ENGLISH], a store, general store, that my dad and mom ran.
And on the other side of our house, we had what they called a big schul.
And the rabbi used to live [INAUDIBLE].
And almost right across the street, we had a [NON-ENGLISH], uptaker, the pharmacist.
The-- the-- the-- the-- slaughterhouse or [NON-ENGLISH] and the pharmacist was the same guy.
He used to have the medicine and everything.
And you went to see the [NON-ENGLISH], and he was also-- meaning the-- the guy who used to kill.
And he was also the-- the town pharmacist, the uptaker as well.
So this was basically our town.
My-- my dad was-- was pretty much active or ran this.
He was the [INAUDIBLE], meaning the forester.
He was in charge of the-- pretty much the mayor without a title.
My dad was a [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was used to take care of the schul, used to take care of the rabbi, used to take care of the-- used to take care of the city, the poor people, any trouble.
In our town, we used to have a lot of trouble with the-- with the gentiles, with the villagers, every so often.
He was the troubleshooter.
He was the troubleshooter there.
If you had a dispute, I owed you money, and I wouldn't pay you, you'd come to [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] used to settle the town.
The stuff.
He was also in charge of the-- of the-- of the forest surrounding-- surrounding.
When the policeman used to come in, I remember, they used to come in town.
They used to stop [INAUDIBLE].
Part of the reason we had a-- had the city store, we had everything around there.
And then he used to-- we had fields, and he used to take care of that.
And the biggest part that I remember of-- of my dad is being all over.
And as a little kid, he used to take me around, the all-- the second kid in the family.
And a boy, he always used to carry me around.
And wherever he went, I used to go-- go with my dad.
But at this time-- and a lot of times I get scared-- I remember my dad's features, but I don't remember his face.
I can describe to you what he-- what he's tall and everything else, but his face is fading away at this time.
But our town-- let me-- going back a little bit, talking about our town and the life.
The life in Trochenbrod was very simple.
We were surrounded by Ukraines and Poles, as I said before.
The villagers and European lifestyle was very rough or dangerous, at least in our shtetl.
But dangerous when it came-- when it came holidays.
For instance, even now, Pesach-- I'll give you an example-- where everybody used to sit, and we'd sing and all this.
We used to close our shutters, close all this.
One of the worst times that we had around Pesach, when you open up the door for [NON-ENGLISH], that's when everybody used to be scared.
Because the Polacks around those time, and the Ukrainians, they used to attack the-- the-- the-- village.
The city.
And most of the time, we'd have a few people would get killed.
The city life was-- one that was-- it was strict.
We would get up in the morning.
We'd go to either to cheder, or we would study.
I mean, that's all we knew in those days, is-- is study.
You have to go to cheder, and you have to study, and you have to work in the field and get ready for Yom Tov.
And you work almost all week to get ready for Shabbos.
And this is the type of life.
And once, twice a week, we would go down to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the-- to the bazaar or market and trade.
But we constantly lived in the fear of-- of being attacked by the Polacks or by the Urkaines.
They used to come down and search.
But we had a very good policeman who used to come in and used to help out a lot.
And my dad was good.
And my grandfather was just about the richest man in town in that time, Katz [INAUDIBLE].
My-- my zaide, when he walked to-- to the-- to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ukraines, to the villages down there, they used to [INAUDIBLE].
He always carried a ax with him.
Matter of fact, when he came to America, he still carried that.
He always carried an ax with him, and he'd just as soon kill you as talk, and they knew that.
My zaide was-- [NON-ENGLISH] was his Jewish name.
And when he walked, you know, the beard with a [NON-ENGLISH], with a black belt and the hat.
He was a religious man.
Everybody there was religious.
But he was deadly when you walked around him.
And he had a lot of land.
He used to deal in-- in cattle, deal in land.
But mostly he would deal with the-- with the-- with the Polacks and the Ukraines in the villages.
As a matter of fact, my dad and mom had a-- a-- a-- store, trading post, in one of the villages outside the city.

And then when I was born, which was already the second child, we moved into-- to Trochenbrod, and we took one of the houses that my grandfather had owned, and that was our house that he turned it over.
Turned it over to us, and my dad bought it from-- from my zaide.
And that's where we lived.
So he knew, zaide knew all the Polacks and all the Ukraines, and used to deal with them.
And he had some good friends.
And he had some that were his enemies, but he knew that whole area, and so did my dad.
Can you describe your household, that is your-- your immediate family and the other members of the household, before the war?
Well, to the best of my recollection, at that time, we had-- there were three of us kids, my brother, which is the youngest.
He's three years younger than I am.
And my sister was a year older than I am.
And myself.
And mom and dad used to be in the house, and we had my grandmother, Tsuppie.
She used to live.
And that's my dad's mother.
In those days, she looked like an old lady.
It's-- it's-- considering the age, you got to-- you've got to think about-- We-- they're saying now, in retrospect, everybody thinks that we were rich in Poland.
I mean, you talk to almost any immigrant, and they say they were rich.
We had bread and potatoes.
We were rich.
We had enough food.
And food was richness in those days.
It was a lot different than richness now.
If you could study, you could sit, you learn.
We considered a rich man if you knew the Gemara, and you knew the-- you could sit down on Shabbos and talk and [YIDDISH] Gemara.
But a rabbi, you were a rich man.
You went to yeshiva.
You came back.
Like my uncle, he was a rich man.
So we-- our house by itself to describe was-- we didn't have any floor.
We used to put on hay on there, and every Friday night used to go to with a rake and clean it up.
And we had-- that was the type of house.
And back of the house, we had our barn.
There was a hothouse and everything else.
And back as far as you could see was our land, and we used to farm it.
We'd raise almost everything.
And the life was real simple.
It wasn't-- it was real relaxed.
I mean, you-- you had people that would come into the house.
You didn't have any locks and keys.
The only thing basic we had to worry is about the goyim to come in and eat or kill you or whatever.
But as far as the city life, was real simple.
In our city, the-- the exception was when somebody was not keeping the Sabbath.
A goy was considered the outcast of the city.
God forbid you don't go to schul on Shabbos, you were the outcast of the [INAUDIBLE].
You know, this guy is-- [INAUDIBLE].
You know, he's-- this was an outcast.
In Europe, in Europe, religion was looked-- at least what I know of, what I have experienced-- was looked a lot different than here.
You were a Jew.
You went to schul.
You practiced Shabbos.
There was no reason not to because everything else was closed on Shabbos.
And there was no reason for you not to be kosher because everything else was kosher.

There was no reason for you not to date a Jewish girl, because everybody else was down there, and you usually didn't because you had a [YIDDISH] And if you had some money, you married your cousin, and your always kept the money in there.
Like with my family, cousins married cousins.
The Szames married the Katzs, and it's been going on for generations.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we used to have-- most that I know of is we'd sit with dad or my [INAUDIBLE] or mom, and they used to tell you about zaides and all this.
My name-- I'm named after my grandfather.
And all going back three generations that I know of, my dad was named after his grandfather, and his grandfather is named after his grandfather.
And all of them were killed, not natural death, but they were killed by the Cossacks or the Bolsheviks and so forth.
My zaide, Itzig, was stabbed on a horse by-- by the Cossacks.
So this has been going on for-- that was no-- no novelty for being killed by the goyim, by the gentiles.
Yeah.

Well, before-- before we get this, what I remember of the beginning of the war is-- and like I said-- we were-- we were in Trochenbrod, and this is in the early '40s, or late-- they started laying cement in part of our street.
Came out where they were going to-- where they were going to pave our street.
In those days, they used cement.
And half-- as matter of fact-- just passed the schul is where they stopped.
So our section of Trochenbrod was already paved.
And we also got-- just got an electric light bulb in our post office, and a telephone, and I remember when they were placing this.
And this must have been in the early '40 or around '41, and it was about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] around Pesach.
Actually, it was almost-- almost around February-- January, February.
Before Pesach.
When the Russians started to retreat, as they were running away, and they were running towards Lutsk and [NON-ENGLISH], which was on the other part, away from the city.
And they were coming in from [NON-ENGLISH], from the upper part.
From the Katowice side.
And they were coming in.

So as the Russians were retreating-- and how I remember that is, you got to picture a little kid coming out and seeing in this-- in those horses, and the walking, and the tanks, and all this going back.
And-- and-- and I remember just seeing the soldiers sitting there.
And you had the horsemen first, and then you had the walkers, and then you had the big kettles that cook and all this.
And they were going back.
And then you had again tanks, and you had more horses, more walkers.
Well, as the Russians were retreating, what they did, they stopped in every home-- and this was early in the morning-- and they knocked on the door.
And what they did is they took all capable man.
And they knew.
They used to go in and take them.
And they were their last rear echelon.
So if you had a rifle, and you had a gun, and you could walk, and you were old enough, they took you as your rear echelon defenders.
And that was when my dad was taken away.
And that must have been around early part of '41, to the best of my recollection, around Pesach time '41.
The reason I remember that is when he left, he had a box of candy that he picked up in Lutsk, and he gave it me.
And he says, here this is for your Pesach, and you kids will have that, when he walked out the door.
And he had his rifle, and that's the last time we seen dad.
We-- we still don't know if he was killed.

But the best of our recollection, months-- it seemed to be years later, but it was actually months-- people were telling us there was a big fight around [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH] was where my uncle used to live.
There was a big battle, and he-- he, being in the rear, or the reason we-- we assume most of the rear echelons that they took from Trochenbrod, those men were killed at that battle.
And that's the way the Russians retreated.
It was real simple.
The soldiers lived while the-- while the flunkies or whatever that were there, they used to take and cover the back, and that gave them time to retreat.
And that's where we figured my dad got killed.
Did we ever find him?
No.
We looked for him.
We had people search for him, but we never found him.
And we just assumed that he got killed at that battle.
To best of my knowledge.
So we lived like that after he left.
The town was not the same.
And I would say it was almost around the summer.
The summer of that '41, when the Germans came in, and this is a nightmare.
Germans came in the town.

And-- and this is-- it's-- you know, when-- when I say, and even when I talk to-- to-- to my kids, or when I look at it, and I think about it, it's-- you couldn't write a situation that lays in one's mind.
And if you could just picture this.
Three Germans, not more, not more, came into the [INAUDIBLE] on the big horses.
Came into town, stopped middle of town, and then you had some other police, Polacks or whatever they were, came in.
And they said, this is the place.
We're going to take headquarters.
And they took headquarters right across the street, and which was at that time, the uptake or whatever you want call it near post office.
And they said, we're taken over.
We're going to be in charge from now on.
You do what we-- what we tell you to do.
And this town is occupied.
Talked in German.
And then they sat down.
And the next thing we knew, they had-- they had some other people.
And the next thing they-- they did, is they brought the rabbi out, which was down the street.
And they brought him up right in the middle.
They called the whole town together.
It was like a town meeting.
And we're staying down there.
We're staying there, and not down there.
But like, like almost, like in front of our house.
And like I said, we already had passed-- the schul was already paved, the street.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]
I don't like to talk about it.
But anyhow, they took the-- the rabbi up.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They shot him and said, this is what we're going to do [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to anybody who doesn't listen.
Or we're going to do-- so we are in charge, and you do what we tell you, unless you end up like this.
Now, we couldn't touch him.
He was laying right down there in his blood.
We couldn't touch him like that for days.
And that was-- that was-- the rabbi-- the rabbi in-- in those days, I-- I don't know if-- most-- most Eastern Europeans would understand what a schtetl was.
A sctetl-- a rabbi and a schtetl was your doctor.
He was your preacher.
He was your-- he was the rabbi.
I mean, he was not necessarily teaching you, or as a rabbi here.
He was not necessarily staying in a schul giving sermons.
He was the rabbi.
When you were sick, you didn't go to a doctor.
You went to the rabbi.
When you were-- when you were well, you didn't go anywhere else.
You went to the rabbi.
He was-- he was the-- the person.
And whatever he said, you know, when he said it's Doomsday, it's Doomsday.
And he said it's day, it's day.
And when they killed this guy, I mean the whole town sort of-- I mean, we were about-- we were about like sheep without-- without a master.
We were just going.
And you've also got to understand that most of our capable men that were around living in that town were also gone.
So what you only had is wives, women, and the children, and-- and the younger people and some older men, who I guess the Russians felt are not capable to being our defenders.
So our city was pretty much what you call in-- in a blase.
It was like sort of Doomsday.
And life-- life was basically you got up in the morning.
You couldn't move too good.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By that time, the Germans had organized.
Now, again, we only had-- in our city, we only had about three, maybe five Germans that were there.
But the best of my knowledge, I can only picture three.
But what they did, they organized all the Poles, all the Ukraines, and a lot of the Jews.
And they were their henchmans, and they took some of the Jewish people, and they made them-- and they gave-- made them positions, big shots.
And what they did, what they picked is a lot of the poor people.
And-- and they made them to be an [GERMAN].
A-- [GERMAN] of the town.
A-- a leader of the city.
And then they-- they organized the villagers.
While it was peaceful in the city, they organized.
Now, some of the organizations, if you can visualize, you have-- and this thing I-- I-- I-- tell a lot of it to my kids in schools when I speak about how easy it is to influence young people.
They used to go-- the Germans went into the villagers, and they took the younger people, about 15, 16, 18-year-old.
I don't think they were more than 21.
And they gave them guns.
They gave them uniforms.
They gave them horses.
And they told them one thing only, go kill Jews.
So we used to have pogroms, attacks, or whatever you want to refer to, almost daily, almost every Saturday or every Yom Tov, you were attacked.
The guys are hooligans, is what we called them.
And they used to ride in town on their horses in groups.
And I probably am exaggerating, but I don't think there were more than about 10, 12, if it was 15, but it seemed like it was a whole battalion.
But I don't think it was more than about that.
And they used to tear the city, basically, apart.
They used to take the women or girls and [INAUDIBLE] revenge from the Jews.
And the only object that they had and what they got paid for is how many they can kill, how many can they rape.
And they used to get rewarded for that.
So if you can picture-- and I use-- I relate to, which I've seen a lot of the Western towns, you have the cattle men coming in the town, shooting it up and all.
That's about what happened to Trochenbrod.
And you could almost figure-- well, when it came around Friday night, we used to try to get together.
Most of us used to get split up.
For an example, we tried not to be too many in one time, because when they attacked, they would used to kill as many as they can.
The younger girls, like my mother, sister, used to always hide Friday night and Saturday.
They used to hide.
Going back, the-- the-- the city were-- the [INAUDIBLE] where we were, almost every home in Trochenbrod-- I don't think it was an exception-- we had storm windows on the outside.
We had hidden cellars or hidden closets.
Almost every house did.
For example, we used to make leather and all this, and when the-- when the goyim used to know, they used to come and attack you, and they'd steal from you.
So we had that.
And then we were afraid from the pogrom days that that's where a lot of Jews survived.
So almost every house had that.
We had a hidden cellar right underneath our kitchen table.
And every house knew it.
And usually the families, if your aunt or your cousin or your uncle had this one, somebody else would know it.
And during those days, most of the-- besides your [NON-ENGLISH], your not dowry.
Whatever you had, your assets-- in those days they said your assets was your silverware, the-- the-- your-- your stuff that you had, your fine dishes.
